ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'tea beverage' class; no specific entry for Straits Classic Tea or Nanyang-style tea exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tea, infusions and other hot beverages
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers tea and hot infusion beverages broadly; no specific Nanyang-style tea code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 refers to tea as a substance; no specific Straits or Nanyang tea concept exists. Closest parent used.

preparation_method: served hot or with condensed milk
allergens: milk (if served with condensed or evaporated milk)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of Straits Classic Tea is approximately 250 ml (250 g), served hot in a traditional kopi tiam ceramic cup or glass.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, black, brewed, prepared with tap water (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 14355)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Straits Classic Tea as served at Toast Box is a Nanyang-style black tea, typically brewed strong and served with condensed milk. The base nutrient values are drawn from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain brewed black tea (per 100 g). The plain-tea values (essentially zero macros) represent the beverage without added milk or sugar. When served with condensed milk, energy and sugar increase substantially; however, the canonical per-100g entry here reflects the plain brewed tea base as the most defensible approved-source anchor. Adaptation noted.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of beverages: plain brewed black tea contains negligible glycemic carbohydrates; GI conventionally assigned as effectively zero
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed black tea contains approximately 0.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g, of which glycemic carbohydrates are negligible. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a specific GI for plain black tea as it contains insufficient carbohydrate to generate a measurable glycemic response. By convention for essentially zero-carbohydrate beverages, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) and gi_category is 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate content per serving is below 1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content in plain brewed tea
hypertension.guidance: Plain Straits Classic Tea contains approximately 3 mg sodium per 100 g and is suitable for individuals managing hypertension; however, if served with condensed milk, sodium and sugar intake increase and portion awareness is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible glycemic carbohydrates in plain brewed tea
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain Straits Classic Tea has a negligible glycemic load and does not raise blood glucose; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request the tea without condensed milk or sugar to avoid added simple sugars that would elevate postprandial glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat and negligible calories in plain brewed tea
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain Straits Classic Tea is essentially calorie-free and fat-free, making it a cardiovascular-neutral beverage; black tea polyphenols (theaflavins and thearubigins) have been associated with modest cardioprotective effects in observational studies, though the clinical significance depends on habitual intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Straits Classic Tea is a traditional Nanyang-style black tea served at Toast Box, a Singaporean kopi tiam chain that recreates the heritage coffeehouse experience of the Straits Settlements era. The tea is brewed from robust black tea leaves — typically a blend of Ceylon and Assam teas — steeped in a cloth sock filter (the traditional 'teh tarik' sock method) to produce a strong, full-bodied infusion. It is commonly served hot with condensed milk and sugar in the classic kopi tiam style, though it may also be ordered 'kosong' (plain, without milk or sugar) or 'siu dai' (less sweet). The name 'Straits' references the historical Straits Settlements of Singapore, Penang, and Malacca, evoking the Peranakan and colonial-era tea culture of the region. Nutritionally, the plain brewed tea base is essentially calorie-free (approximately 1 kcal per 100 g), with negligible protein, fat, and carbohydrate. It contains trace minerals including potassium (37 mg/100 g) and small amounts of folate. The beverage is rich in polyphenolic antioxidants, particularly theaflavins and thearubigins from black tea oxidation, which have been studied for anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective properties. When served with condensed milk, the caloric and sugar content increases substantially. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease, the plain version is a safe and appropriate beverage choice. The glycemic index of plain brewed black tea is effectively zero due to negligible glycemic carbohydrate content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Straits Classic Tea?
Is Straits Classic Tea suitable for diabetics?
Is Straits tea the same as teh tarik?
Can I drink Straits Classic Tea if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of black tea?
Does Straits Classic Tea contain milk?

confidence_reason: Straits Classic Tea is a branded menu item from Toast Box (field survey data). The nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for plain brewed black tea, which is the most defensible approved-source anchor available. No HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists specifically for this branded item. Confidence is medium because the base tea values are well-supported but the exact preparation (with or without condensed milk) affects the nutritional profile significantly, and the branded formulation is not independently verified in an approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values reflect plain brewed black tea per 100 g, sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 14355 / FDC ID 171917). When served with condensed milk and sugar as is typical at Toast Box, energy_kcal, carbohydrate_g, sugar_g, and fat_g will be substantially higher. A separate record for 'Teh Tarik' or 'Teh C' with milk would better capture the as-served nutritional profile. 2. protein_g is naturally zero as plain brewed black tea contains no protein by nature. fat_g is naturally zero as plain brewed black tea contains no fat by nature. 3. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate per 250 g serving is approximately 0.75 g, below the threshold for meaningful glycemic load calculation. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg, and calcium_mg are 0.0 as confirmed zero or below detection threshold per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for plain brewed black tea; confirmed zero per approved source. 5. Price data (SGD 10.60) from field survey input likely reflects a set meal or bundle rather than a single cup of tea; this field is not carried into the RESULT template as it is not part of the nutritional record structure. 6. The -1 sentinel value is not used in this record as USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy provides reported values (including zeros) for all listed nutrients for plain brewed black tea. All numeric fields are set to their reported or confirmed values.
